content:
# Mark Chapter 2 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Mark Chapter 2 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 2:1 — "returned to" vs. "having-entered again into" for εἰσελθὼν πάλιν εἰς

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And having-entered again into Capernaum after [some] days, it-was-heard that he-is in [a] house." | Preserves participle structure and indefinite "a house." |
| LOGOS | "When he returned to Capernaum after some days, it was reported that he was at home." | Smooths to natural English; renders ἐν οἴκῳ as "at home." |
| PNEUMA | "A few days later, Jesus returned to Capernaum, and word got around that he was home." | Conversational; adds "Jesus" as subject for clarity; idiomatic "word got around." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "at home" (with LOGOS/PNEUMA) over "in a house" — ἐν οἴκῳ most likely means "at home" in this context. Used "word spread" as a middle register between "it-was-heard" and "word got around."

**Status:** Resolved. Minor stylistic divergence; "at home" accepted as contextually correct.

---

## 2:2 — Structural differences in crowd description

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "so-that no-longer was-there-room, not-even the [space] toward the door" | Hyper-literal; preserves Greek word order and brackets implied words. |
| LOGOS | "there was no longer room for them, not even around the door" | Formal equivalent with natural English phrasing. |
| PNEUMA | "So many people packed into the house that there was no room left—not even outside the door" | Dynamic; adds "packed into the house" for vividness; "outside" interprets πρός. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's balanced phrasing with "not even at the door" — faithful to πρὸς τὴν θύραν without over-interpreting.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "packed into" is vivid but adds to the source.

---

## 2:3–4 — κράβαττος: "pallet" vs. "mat" vs. "stretcher"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "pallet" | Technical term preserving the colloquial register of the Greek word. |
| LOGOS | "mat" | Clear, accessible English for a portable sleeping surface. |
| PNEUMA | "stretcher" | Emphasizes the carrying function in the narrative context. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "mat" throughout (vv. 4, 9, 11, 12). κράβαττος refers to a simple, portable sleeping mat used by the poor — "pallet" is archaic, "stretcher" implies medical equipment that doesn't match the ancient context.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Mat" is accurate and accessible.

---

## 2:4 — "they-unroofed the roof" vs. "removed the roof" vs. "tore it open"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "they-unroofed the roof where he-was, and having-dug-through, they-lower the pallet" | Preserves Mark's redundant ἀπεστέγασαν τὴν στέγην. |
| LOGOS | "they removed the roof above where he was, and having broken through, they lowered the mat" | Smooths the redundancy; "broken through" for ἐξορύξαντες. |
| PNEUMA | "they climbed up on the roof above him, tore it open, and lowered the stretcher" | Adds "climbed up" (implied); "tore it open" for dramatic effect. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "removed the roof above where he was, and having dug through it, they lowered the mat." Follows LOGOS's clarity but retains "dug through" (closer to ἐξορύξαντες, which implies digging through packed earth).

**Status:** Resolved. Balanced between LOGOS and EMET.

---

## 2:5 — "Child" vs. "My child" for τέκνον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Child" | Direct translation of τέκνον. |
| LOGOS | "Child" | Same as EMET. |
| PNEUMA | "My child" | Adds possessive for warmth and pastoral intimacy. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Child" without the possessive. The Greek has no possessive pronoun; the tenderness is already in the word τέκνον itself.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's addition, while emotionally apt, goes beyond the text.

---

## 2:6–7 — "scribes" vs. "religious scholars"; structural divergence

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "some of-the scribes...reasoning in their hearts...'He-blasphemes! Who is-able to-forgive sins except one — God?'" | Literal structure; "scribes" preserved. |
| LOGOS | "some of the scribes were sitting there, reasoning in their hearts...'He blasphemes! Who can forgive sins but God alone?'" | Formal; retains "scribes." |
| PNEUMA | "Some religious scholars were sitting nearby, and in their minds they were thinking...'This is blasphemy! Only God has the power to forgive sins.'" | "Religious scholars" for γραμματεῖς; restructures the rhetorical question into a declaration. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Retained "scribes" as a well-established term; kept the rhetorical question format as in the Greek. PNEUMA's conversion of the question to a statement changes the rhetorical force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:8 — "perceiving in his spirit" vs. "having-known in his spirit" vs. "sensed"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having-known in his spirit" (ἐπιγνούς τῷ πνεύματι αὐτοῦ) | Literal; preserves "known" for ἐπιγνούς. |
| LOGOS | "perceiving in his spirit" | Formal equivalent; "perceiving" for the participle. |
| PNEUMA | "immediately sensed what they were debating in their minds" | Dynamic; "sensed" and "debating" interpret freely. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "perceiving in his spirit" — it conveys the intuitive knowledge implied by ἐπιγνούς without the overly cognitive "known" or the reductive "sensed."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:11 — "go home" vs. "go to your house" — word order and register

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "To-you I-say: Rise, take-up your pallet, and go to your house." | Preserves fronted dative σοὶ λέγω. |
| LOGOS | "I say to you: Rise, take up your mat, and go to your home." | Standard English word order; "home" for οἶκον. |
| PNEUMA | "I'm telling you: Get up, pick up your stretcher, and go home." | Colloquial; contractions; "get up" and "pick up" for urgency. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "I say to you, rise, take up your mat, and go home." Blends LOGOS's dignity with the concise "go home."

**Status:** Resolved. Stylistic divergence only.

---

## 2:12 — "We have never seen anything like this" vs. "Thus we-never saw"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Thus we-never saw." | Hyper-literal rendering of οὕτως οὐδέποτε εἴδαμεν. |
| LOGOS | "We have never seen anything like this!" | Adds "anything like this" to complete the English idiom. |
| PNEUMA | "We have never seen anything like this!" | Same as LOGOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted the fuller English rendering "We have never seen anything like this!" The Greek ellipsis needs completion in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:15 — "his house" vs. "Levi's house" — referent clarification

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he-reclines in his house" | Preserves ambiguous pronoun from Greek (αὐτοῦ). |
| LOGOS | "he was reclining at table in his house" | Same ambiguous pronoun retained. |
| PNEUMA | "while Jesus was having dinner at Levi's house" | Specifies "Levi's house" to resolve ambiguity; "having dinner" for reclining. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Used "in Levi's house" to resolve the pronoun ambiguity — context makes clear this is Levi's home, and clarity serves the reader without distorting meaning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of PNEUMA's clarification.

---

## 2:16 — "scribes of the Pharisees" vs. "Pharisees' legal experts"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the scribes of-the Pharisees" | Direct rendering of οἱ γραμματεῖς τῶν Φαρισαίων. |
| LOGOS | "the scribes of the Pharisees" | Same as EMET. |
| PNEUMA | "the Pharisees' legal experts" | Interprets γραμματεῖς as "legal experts" for modern accessibility. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Retained "scribes of the Pharisees" — "scribes" is the established term and preserves the important distinction from "scribes and Pharisees."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 2:17 — "Those who are well" vs. "The-ones being-strong" vs. "Healthy people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The-ones being-strong have no need of-a-physician, but the-ones having badly." | Literal; "being-strong" for ἰσχύοντες; retains Greek idiom "having badly." |
| LOGOS | "Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick." | Formal equivalent; natural English. |
| PNEUMA | "Healthy people don't need a doctor—sick people do. I didn't come to call those who think they're righteous." | Dynamic; adds "who think they're" — interpretive gloss on the irony. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's balanced rendering. PNEUMA's "who think they're righteous" makes the irony explicit, but the Greek leaves it implicit — the reader should discern it.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's interpretive addition noted but not adopted.

---

## 2:19 — υἱοὶ τοῦ νυμφῶνος: "sons of the bridal chamber" vs. "wedding guests"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "sons of-the-bridal-chamber" | Literal Semitic idiom preserved in English. |
| LOGOS | "wedding guests" | Idiomatic English equivalent. |
| PNEUMA | "wedding guests" | Same as LOGOS; restructures as rhetorical question with "Of course not." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "wedding guests" — the Semitic idiom requires translation into a functional English equivalent. "Sons of the bridal chamber" is opaque to modern readers.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 2:21 — Garment/patch analogy — structural differences

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the fullness takes-away from it — the new from the old — and a-worse tear comes-into-being" | Literal; preserves τὸ πλήρωμα as "the fullness." |
| LOGOS | "the patch pulls away from it—the new from the old—and a worse tear results" | Clear English; "patch" for πλήρωμα in context. |
| PNEUMA | "the new patch will pull away from the old cloth and leave an even bigger hole" | Dynamic paraphrase; "bigger hole" for "worse tear." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "patch" correctly interprets πλήρωμα in this tailoring context, and the dash construction preserves Mark's explanatory aside.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:22 — Wineskins conclusion: elliptical vs. expanded

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But new wine into new wineskins." | Preserves the verbless Greek clause. |
| LOGOS | "But new wine is for new wineskins." | Supplies copula "is for" to complete the English sentence. |
| PNEUMA | "No—fresh wine goes into fresh wineskins." | Dynamic; "fresh" for νέος; adds verb "goes." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "New wine is for new wineskins" — the ellipsis needs a verb in English, and "is for" is minimal and faithful.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:23 — ὁδὸν ποιεῖν: "to make a way" vs. "made their way" vs. "walking through"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his disciples began to-make [a] way, plucking the heads-of-grain" | Preserves the ambiguous ὁδὸν ποιεῖν. |
| LOGOS | "his disciples began to pluck heads of grain as they made their way" | Interprets as "make their way" (travel). |
| PNEUMA | "the disciples began picking heads of grain as they went" | Simplifies; drops the "make a way" phrase. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "began to pluck heads of grain as they walked" — interprets ὁδὸν ποιεῖν as describing movement (the most natural reading), while using "walked" to link with the grain field setting.

**Status:** Resolved. The phrase's ambiguity is noted in EMET's textual notes.

---

## 2:26 — "bread of the Presence" vs. "bread of-the-presentation" vs. "sacred bread"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "bread of-the-presentation" | Literal for ἄρτους τῆς προθέσεως. |
| LOGOS | "bread of the Presence" | Traditional English rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "the sacred bread that only priests were allowed to eat" | Dynamic; explains the bread's nature rather than naming it. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "bread of the Presence" — the traditional term is widely recognized and accurately renders the concept. PNEUMA's explanatory approach loses the specific OT designation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:27 — "came-into-being for the sake of man" vs. "was made for man" vs. "was made to serve people"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The Sabbath came-into-being for the sake of man, and not man for the sake of the Sabbath." | Literal; preserves ἐγένετο and διά + accusative. |
| LOGOS | "The Sabbath was made for man, and not man for the Sabbath." | Formal equivalent; classic English rendering. |
| PNEUMA | "The Sabbath was made to serve people—people were not made to serve the Sabbath." | Dynamic; adds "serve" to make the functional point explicit. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "was made for man" is both faithful and memorable. PNEUMA's "serve" captures the implication but adds interpretive content.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 2:28 — "So-that lord is" vs. "Therefore...is lord" vs. "That is why...has authority over"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "So-that lord is the Son of Man even of-the Sabbath." | Literal Greek word order with ὥστε. |
| LOGOS | "Therefore the Son of Man is lord even of the Sabbath." | Standard English; "Therefore" for ὥστε. |
| PNEUMA | "That is why the Son of Man has authority over even the Sabbath." | Dynamic; "has authority over" interprets κύριος. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering — "lord" preserves the title κύριος, and "Therefore" correctly renders the inferential ὥστε.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **Vocabulary: archaic/literal vs. accessible** (e.g., pallet/mat/stretcher, scribes/religious scholars) | vv. 3-4, 6, 16, 26 | Favor LOGOS's balanced register |
| **Semitic idiom translation** (sons of bridal chamber, bread of Presence) | vv. 19, 26 | Translate the idiom into functional English |
| **PNEUMA's interpretive additions** (possessives, explanatory glosses) | vv. 5, 15, 17 | Generally not adopted; preserve the text's own restraint |
| **Elliptical Greek requiring English completion** | vv. 12, 22 | Supply minimal words to complete English sense |
| **Structural/word order differences** | vv. 1, 2, 11, 28 | Follow natural English order (LOGOS) |
| **EMET's hyper-literal constructions** | Throughout | Appreciated for transparency; rarely adopted as final rendering |

**Overall Pattern Analysis:** Mark 2 shows the expected three-way divergence pattern. EMET consistently preserves Greek syntax and vocabulary at the expense of English readability. LOGOS provides the most balanced formal-equivalent renderings and was the most frequently adopted base text. PNEUMA captures narrative energy and emotional force but tends to add interpretive content (e.g., "who think they're righteous" in v. 17, "My child" in v. 5) that goes beyond the source text. SOPHIA's consensus generally favors LOGOS's middle register, incorporating PNEUMA's clarifications only when they resolve genuine ambiguity (e.g., "Levi's house" in v. 15) and EMET's precision when it preserves important nuance (e.g., "dug through" in v. 4).
